zh.lifeSummary: 當Sir Albert Richard Perry Of Kirkton於1480年出生在英國英格蘭戴文艾可謝特時，他的父親William Perry III14歲，母親Joane Holcombe12歲。他和Agnes Stalworth至少有5個兒子和1個女兒。他去世於1594年在他的家鄉享年114歲，埋葬在英國林可爾史爾吉爾頓 在 林賽。

zh-hans.lifeSummary: 当Sir Albert Richard Perry Of Kirkton于1480年出生在英国英格兰戴文艾可謝特时，他的父亲William Perry III14岁，母亲Joane Holcombe12岁。他和Agnes Stalworth至少有5个儿子和1个女儿。他去世于1594年在他的家乡享年114岁，埋葬在英国林可爾史爾吉爾頓 在 林賽。

nameMeanings.name: Perry
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Welsh: Anglicized form of Welsh ap Herry ‘son of Herry’, a variant of the personal name Harry (see Harris ).

English: topographic name for someone who lived near a pear tree (Middle English piri(e), peri(e), puri(e), Old English pirige, pyrige), or a habitational name for someone who owned or came from a place so named. This surname and a number of variants have been established in Ireland since the 17th century.

variants.name: Peery
meanings: Irish: variant of   Perry  , taken to Ireland at the time of thePlantation of Ulster and in the Cromwellian period.

variants.name: Pear
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Jewish Dov.
English: topographic name from residence by a pear tree (Middle English pere, Old English peru, pere). Compare Perry .
English: nickname from Middle English pere, Old French per, peer ‘peer or paragon’, also ‘companion’.

variants.name: Peary
meanings: English (mainly northern): variant of   Perry  .

History: Robert E. Peary (1856–1920) was a U.S.-born explorer, who in 1909 led the first successful expedition to the North Pole. Peary Land in the north of Greenland is named after him, as he explored there in 1892 and 1900.

variants.name: Perrie
meanings: Scottish: variant of Perry 2. Alternatively, perhaps from the Old French personal name Pierre; see Pierce .

variants.name: Harris
meanings: English (southern England and south Wales): from the personal name Harry + genitival -s. This surname is also established in Ireland, taken there principally during the Plantation of Ulster. However, in some cases, particularly in families coming from County Mayo, Harris can be an Anglicized form of Gaelic Ó hEarchadha. This surname is also very common among African Americans.

American shortened and altered form of Greek surnames begining with Cha(r)-, such as Chasandrinos (variant of Kassandrinos, a habitational name from the Kassandra peninsula of Chalkidiki), and various patronymics from the personal name Charalampos (see Charos ). In North America, the surname Harris may possibly also originate from a transferred use of the Greek personal (given) name Charis or Harris (shortened forms of Charalampos) as a surname (i.e. as a replacement of the original surname).
Americanized form of various like-sounding Jewish surnames.

variants.name: Perryman
meanings: English (Devon): elaborated form of Perry , with Middle English man, probably denoting one who lived near a pear tree; see Pearman .

nameMeanings.name: Of
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Chinese: Cheng, Jing, Kam Fung, Wai Shing, Wing, Yali, Yuk.
Chinese:
variant Romanization of the surnames 歐 and 區, see   Ou   1 and 2.

variants.name: Ou
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Chinese Wei, Ying, Ching, Heng, Hong, Jia, Kuang Yu, Li-Ching, Cho, Chong, Chung, Sa.  Southeast Asian Lichun, Ming, Quan, Roeun, Sokhom, Chau, Hai, Leang, Ly, Nhanh, Phan, Pho, Tha.

Chinese: Mandarin form of the surname 歐, meaning ‘to sing someone's praise’ in ancient Chinese: (i) from the placename Dong Ou (東甌), a fief (in present-day Wenzhou in Zhejiang province) which was granted to Yao in 192  BC . It is said that Yao was a descendant of Wujiang (reigned 355–333  BC ), king of the state of Yue (located mainly in present-day Zhejiang and Jiangxi provinces) during the Spring and Autumn period (770–476  BC ). (ii) from the placename Ou Yu (歐餘), the name of a fief (located in present-day Zhejiang province) that was granted to Ti, the second son of Wujiang (reigned 355–333  BC ), king of the state of Yue (located mainly in present-day Zhejiang and Jiangxi provinces) during the Spring and Autumn period (770–476  BC ).

Chinese: Mandarin form of the surname 區, originally denoting an ancient vessel for measurement in ancient Chinese: from the personal name of Ou Zhizi (歐治子), an expert sword-maker who lived during the Spring and Autumn period (770–476 BC ). Some of his descendants inherited Ou (歐) as their surname, simplifying the Chinese character 歐 to 區.

nameMeanings.name: Kirkton
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of Family Names in Britain and Ireland © University of the West of England 2016
meanings: Scottish: locative name from any of numerous places in Scotland so called, named as ‘church settlement’.

nameMeanings.name: Sir
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: equivalent of George .

variants.name: George
meanings: Via Old French and Latin, from Greek Georgios (a derivative of geōrgos ‘farmer’, from gē ‘earth’ + ergein ‘to work’). This was the name of several early saints, including the shadowy figure who is now the patron of England (as well as of Germany and Portugal). If the saint existed at all, he was perhaps martyred in Palestine in the persecutions of Christians instigated by the Emperor Diocletian at the beginning of the 4th century. The popular legend in which the hero slays a dragon is a medieval Italian invention. He was for a long time a more important saint in the Orthodox Church than in the West, and the name was not much used in England during the Middle Ages, even after St George came to be regarded as the patron of England in the 14th century. Its use increased from the 1400s, and by 1500 it was regularly among the most popular male names. This popularity was reinforced when George I came to the throne in 1714 , bringing this name with him from Germany. It has been one of the most popular English boys' names ever since.

nameMeanings.name: Albert
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: From an Old French name, Albert, of Germanic (Frankish) origin, derived from adal ‘noble’ + berht ‘bright, famous’. This was adopted by the Normans and introduced by them to England, displacing the Old English form Æþelbeorht. The name is popular in a variety of forms in Western Europe, and has been traditional in a number of European princely families. It was out of favour in England for centuries, however, and the revival of its popularity in the 19th century was largely in honour of Queen Victoria's consort, Prince Albert of Saxe-Coburg-Gotha .

nameMeanings.name: Richard
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: One of the most enduringly successful of the Old French personal names introduced into Britain by the Normans. It is of Germanic (Frankish) origin, derived from rīc ‘power’ + hard ‘strong, hardy’. It has enjoyed continuous popularity in England from the Conquest to the present day, influenced by the fact that it was borne by three kings of England, in particular Richard I ( 1157–99 ). He was king for only ten years ( 1189–99 ), most of which he spent in warfare abroad, taking part in the Third Crusade and costing the people of England considerable sums in taxes. Nevertheless, he achieved the status of a folk hero, and was never in England long enough to disappoint popular faith in his goodness and justice. He was also Duke of Aquitaine and Normandy and Count of Anjou, fiefs which he held at a time of maximum English expansion in France. His exploits as a leader of the Third Crusade earned him the nickname ‘Coeur de Lion’ or ‘Lionheart’ and a permanent place in popular imagination, in which he was even more firmly enshrined by Sir Walter Scott's novel Ivanhoe ( 1820 ).

variants.name: Rikert
meanings: One of the most enduringly successful of the Old French personal names introduced into Britain by the Normans. It is of Germanic (Frankish) origin, derived from rīc ‘power’ + hard ‘strong, hardy’. It has enjoyed continuous popularity in England from the Conquest to the present day, influenced by the fact that it was borne by three kings of England, in particular Richard I ( 1157–99 ). He was king for only ten years ( 1189–99 ), most of which he spent in warfare abroad, taking part in the Third Crusade and costing the people of England considerable sums in taxes. Nevertheless, he achieved the status of a folk hero, and was never in England long enough to disappoint popular faith in his goodness and justice. He was also Duke of Aquitaine and Normandy and Count of Anjou, fiefs which he held at a time of maximum English expansion in France. His exploits as a leader of the Third Crusade earned him the nickname ‘Coeur de Lion’ or ‘Lionheart’ and a permanent place in popular imagination, in which he was even more firmly enshrined by Sir Walter Scott's novel Ivanhoe ( 1820 ).

variants.name: Rickert
meanings: One of the most enduringly successful of the Old French personal names introduced into Britain by the Normans. It is of Germanic (Frankish) origin, derived from rīc ‘power’ + hard ‘strong, hardy’. It has enjoyed continuous popularity in England from the Conquest to the present day, influenced by the fact that it was borne by three kings of England, in particular Richard I ( 1157–99 ). He was king for only ten years ( 1189–99 ), most of which he spent in warfare abroad, taking part in the Third Crusade and costing the people of England considerable sums in taxes. Nevertheless, he achieved the status of a folk hero, and was never in England long enough to disappoint popular faith in his goodness and justice. He was also Duke of Aquitaine and Normandy and Count of Anjou, fiefs which he held at a time of maximum English expansion in France. His exploits as a leader of the Third Crusade earned him the nickname ‘Coeur de Lion’ or ‘Lionheart’ and a permanent place in popular imagination, in which he was even more firmly enshrined by Sir Walter Scott's novel Ivanhoe ( 1820 ).

historicEvents.title: Book of Common Prayer
historicEvents.description: Book of Common Prayer, a product of the English Reformation, was published in 1549 for assistance in the administration of the sacraments and other rites and ceremonies of the church according to the use of the Church of England. The book outlined morning, evening, and communion prayers and orders for baptism and marriage, thus making England a truly Protestant state.

historicEvents.title: Act of Uniformity
historicEvents.description: The Act of Uniformity was passed by the Parliament of England and required all people to go to church once a week. The consequence of not attending church was a fine of 12 pence, which was a considerable amount for a poor person.

historicEvents.title: State Lottery
historicEvents.description: A State Lottery was recorded in 1569. The tickets were sold at St. Paul's Cathedral in London.

historicEvents.title: The Royal Exchange is Organized
historicEvents.description: The Royal Exchange was organized in January 1571 by Sir Thomas Gresham. Gresham is known as the father of English banking.

en.lifeSummary: When Sir Albert Richard Perry Of Kirkton was born in 1480, in Exeter, Devon, England, United Kingdom, his father, William Perry III, was 14 and his mother, Joane Holcombe, was 12. He had at least 5 sons and 1 daughter with Agnes Stalworth. He died in 1594, in his hometown, at the age of 114, and was buried in Kirton in Lindsey, Lincolnshire, England.

fr.lifeSummary: Lorsque Sir Albert Richard Perry Of Kirkton est né en 1480, à Exeter, Devon, Angleterre, Royaume-Uni, son père, William Perry III, avait 14 ans et sa mère, Joane Holcombe, avait 12 ans. Il a eu au moins 5 fils et 1 fille avec Agnes Stalworth. Il est décédé en 1594, dans sa ville natale, à l’âge de 114 ans, et a été enterré à Kirton in Lindsey, Lincolnshire, Angleterre.

de.lifeSummary: Als Sir Albert Richard Perry Of Kirkton 1480 in Exeter, Devon, England, United Kingdom geboren wurde, war sein Vater William Perry III 14 und seine Mutter Joane Holcombe 12. Er hatte mit Agnes Stalworth mindestens 5 Söhne und 1 Tochter. Er starb 1594 im Alter von 114 Jahren in seinem Geburtsort und wurde in Kirton in Lindsey, Lincolnshire, England begraben.

it.lifeSummary: Quando Sir Albert Richard Perry Of Kirkton è nato il nel 1480 nel Exeter, Devon, Inghilterra, Regno Unito, il suo padre, William Perry III, aveva 14 anni e il suo madre, Joane Holcombe, aveva 12 anni. Lui avuto almeno 5 figli e 1 figlia con Agnes Stalworth. Morì nel 1594, nel sua cittadina, all'età di 114 anni, e fu sepolto a Kirton in Lindsey, Lincolnshire, England.

ja.lifeSummary: Sir Albert Richard Perry Of Kirktonが1480年にイギリスイギリスデボンエクセターで生まれた時，父William Perry IIIは14歳，母Joane Holcombeは12歳であった。彼はAgnes Stalworthとの間に少なくとも5人の息子と1人の娘をもうけた。彼は1594年に故郷で114歳で死去し，イギリスリンコルンシュアイアキルトン イン リンドセイにて埋葬された。

ko.lifeSummary: Sir Albert Richard Perry Of Kirkton(이)가 1480년 영국 잉글랜드 데폰 세터에서 태어났을 때, 그의 아버지 William Perry III(은)는 14세였고, 그의 어머니 Joane Holcombe(은)는 12세였다. 그는 Agnes Stalworth(와)과 적어도 다섯 명의 아들과 한 명의 딸을 두었다. 그는 1594년 그의 고향에서 114세의 나이로 사망했으며 영국 링코실르 커턴 인 린지에 묻혔다.

pt.lifeSummary: Quando Sir Albert Richard Perry Of Kirkton nasceu em 1480, em Exeter, Devon, Inglaterra, United Kingdom, seu pai, William Perry III, tinha 14 anos e sua mãe, Joane Holcombe, tinha 12 anos. Ele teve pelo menos 5 filhos e 1 filha com Agnes Stalworth. Ele faleceu em 1594, em sua cidade natal, com 114 anos, e foi sepultado em Kirton in Lindsey, Lincolnshire, Inglaterra.

ru.lifeSummary: Когда Sir Albert Richard Perry Of Kirkton родился в 1480 г. в местности эксетир, девон, Англия, Великобритания, его отцу по имени William Perry III было 14 лет, а матери, которую звали Joane Holcombe, 12. У него было по крайней мере 5 сыновей и 1 дочь от Agnes Stalworth. Он умер в 1594 г. в его родном городе в возрасте 114 лет и был похоронен в местности киртон ин линдси, линколншир, Англия.

es.lifeSummary: Cuando Sir Albert Richard Perry Of Kirkton nació en 1480, en Exeter, Devon, Inglaterra, Reino Unido, su papá, William Perry III, tenía 14 años y su mamá, Joane Holcombe, tenía 12 años. Tuvo por lo menos 5 hijos y 1 hija con Agnes Stalworth. Murió en 1594, en su pueblo, a la edad de 114 años, y fue sepultado en Kirton in Lindsey, Lincolnshire, Inglaterra.
